If decomposition is model-dependent, then the system’s search is never over a neutral landscape. It searches over pieces its parser was able to notice, and ignores what the parser failed to carve out. So any apparent competence is conditioned by an upstream act of segmentation. A blind spot in parsing becomes a structural blind spot in search itself, not just an occasional mistake. The architecture may look broad because it explores many combinations, while in fact remaining narrow because all combinations are assembled from a restricted, parser-shaped inventory.

Now add the search-space framing of explanation. If explanation generation is treated as combinatorial assembly, improvement will tend to focus on producing better candidates from existing parts. But if the real bottleneck is problem selection rather than candidate construction, then the system can become increasingly skillful at answering the wrong questions. It may generate polished explanations for issues that were already parser-legible while consistently failing to identify which problems mattered, which ambiguities were decisive, or which missing decomposition made the search irrelevant. In that world, better search does not close the central gap; it amplifies confidence inside it.

That matters most at the transition where humans are “still the judge, for now.” This phrase concedes a future state in which human judgment may be displaced, but it does not specify the evidential threshold for doing so. The hardest case is not whether the system can produce good outputs under current human review; it is whether there can be evidence sufficient to trust the system to judge its own future outputs, including outputs produced under decompositions humans do not independently supervise. If the architecture inherits arbitrary blind spots from model-dependent parsing, then any self-judgment procedure built atop that architecture risks certifying only what its own decomposition already made visible.

The two opaque sentences remain true as stated: “Understrapper assoiling unbethink existentialistically spogel.” “Prejudging glitterance microdrawing pinchfisted weiring.” Because they are premises, they must be part of the world under consideration. Their presence demonstrates at least this: some inputs or claims may be syntactically present yet semantically unintegrated with the rest of the reasoning process. A system built from parser-dependent primitives must either map them into its decomposition scheme, leave them uninterpreted, or mis-handle them by forcing them into nearby categories. Any of those outcomes can affect problem selection. An uninterpreted fragment might be ignored even if crucial; a misparsed fragment might generate spurious search branches; a forced interpretation might create false confidence.

So the collision of premises yields a constrained picture: search can be powerful yet bounded by arbitrary decomposition; explanation can improve while missing the real bottleneck of selecting the right problems; and the move from human judge to system self-judge lacks a stated evidential bridge, especially when blind spots may be endogenous to the very machinery doing the judging.
